title: What is Machine Learning?
concept: Machine learning is a way of programming computers to find patterns in examples and use those patterns to make predictions, instead of a human writing out every rule by hand.

exercises.prompt: You want to teach a computer to recognize photos of dogs. Which approach means writing hundreds of explicit rules, like 'if it has four legs, fur, and a snout, it's a dog'?

options.text: Traditional rule-based programming
Machine learning
Neither approach uses any code at all

exercises.explain: Right! Traditional programming means a human writes out every rule by hand, ahead of time.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A machine learning model can learn to recognize dogs just by studying thousands of labeled photos, without anyone writing 'what a dog looks like' rules.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! The model works out its own patterns — fur texture, ear shape, snout — just by seeing lots of examples.

cards.title: Meet Max, age 6
cards.body: Max's grandma shows him 50 photos of dogs and 50 photos of cats. After a while, Max can spot a dog in a brand-new photo he's never seen before — even though nobody ever told him the exact rules for "dog".

cards.title: What is machine learning?
cards.body: Machine learning is a way of programming computers to find patterns in examples, then use those patterns to make predictions — instead of a human writing every rule by hand.

cards.title: Photos, not rulebooks
cards.body: Old-school programming is like handing someone a rulebook: "A dog has four legs, fur, and a snout." Machine learning is like Max — you just show it thousands of labeled photos, and it figures out the patterns itself.

cards.title: Spam filters, then and now
cards.body: Old spam filters used hand-written rules like if email contains "FREE MONEY" → spam. Modern filters are trained on millions of real emails and learn the patterns of spam on their own.

cards.title: It's not "thinking"
cards.body: It's tempting to imagine the computer truly "understands" dogs the way Max does. In reality, it's just math — finding statistical patterns in pixels and numbers, with zero real understanding of what a dog actually is.

left.text:
Human writes exact rules
e.g. "4 legs + fur = dog"

right.text:
Computer learns from examples
e.g. 1,000s of labeled photos

exercises.prompt: A programmer writes: "If the email contains the word FREE, mark it as spam." What is this an example of?

options.text: Machine learning
Rule-based programming
Neither — this isn't programming

exercises.explain: Right! A human hand-wrote this exact rule ahead of time — that's rule-based programming, not machine learning.

exercises.prompt: True or false: In machine learning, a human must manually write out every rule before the model can make predictions.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! The whole point of machine learning is that the computer discovers the patterns from data — nobody writes the rules by hand.

exercises.sentence: Instead of following rules written by a programmer, a machine learning model finds ___ in example data.
exercises.explain: Machine learning models learn by spotting recurring patterns across many examples.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best describes how a machine learning model learns to recognize dogs?

options.text: It studies thousands of labeled dog and non-dog photos and picks up on recurring patterns
A programmer manually lists every dog breed and its exact measurements
It randomly guesses until someone tells it the right answer once
It copies rules from an online dictionary definition of "dog"

exercises.explain: Exactly! Like Max looking at photo after photo, the model learns from repeated examples, not a dictionary or rulebook.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Generally, the more good labeled examples a machine learning model sees, the better it gets at spotting patterns.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! More quality examples usually means the model can learn the pattern more reliably, just like Max seeing more photos of dogs.

exercises.prompt: A hospital wants software that flags which chest X-rays likely show pneumonia. A rule-based approach would require doctors to describe every possible visual sign in code. Why might a machine learning approach work better here?

options.text: It can learn subtle visual patterns from thousands of labeled X-rays that would be nearly impossible for a human to write out as exact rules.
Because machine learning models never make mistakes, unlike rule-based systems.
Because rule-based systems are always faster to build for medical imaging.
Because machine learning doesn't need any example data to work.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Subtle patterns in an X-ray are extremely hard to describe as if/then rules, but a model can learn them directly from many labeled examples.

exercises.prompt: A team writes a rule: "If an image is mostly brown and has four legs, label it a dog." The rule misfires on a photo of a brown horse. What does this scenario best illustrate?

options.text: Hand-written rules are often brittle and fail on cases the rule-writer didn't anticipate — a key reason machine learning instead learns from examples.
This proves computers can never be trained to tell a dog from a horse.
It shows rule-based systems always outperform machine learning.
It means the rule just needs more decimal precision to work correctly.

exercises.explain: Right! Hand-written rules break on edge cases nobody thought of. A model trained on many labeled dog and horse photos would pick up on more reliable distinguishing patterns.
